paper_title: Matched-Budget Evaluation of Weak-View Residuals for One-Run Differential Privacy Auditing
email_title: Matched-Budget Evaluation of Weak-View Residuals for One-Run Differential Privacy Auditing

sections.summary: This paper investigates whether repeated weak, label-preserving test-time queries provide additional empirical privacy evidence in one-run, final-model-only DP auditing once all queries are honestly accounted for. Building on the quantile-regression calibrated single-view baseline of Liu et al. (2025), the authors propose a public-data-only weak-view residual score that aggregates clean, worst-view, and agreement residuals, and evaluate it under a matched-total-query protocol on Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 at target ε ≈ 4 and 8. Across four seeds, fixed audit pools (240 pairs/checkpoint), and budgets from 40–1200 queries, the central finding is negative: the structured weak-view score does not improve the empirical ε lower bound at δ = 10⁻⁵ over the clean quantile baseline in any setting.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The paper isolates a focused and practically relevant question: do weak multi-views add privacy evidence beyond a strong, calibrated single-view baseline once queries are charged?
Introduces a clear, auditable weak-view residual scoring formulation Sstructured = max{rclean, rworst, λ ragree} entirely calibrated on public data, without altering the one-run auditing and privacy conversion pipeline.
Proposes a principled matched-total-query protocol to compare methods fairly by consumed queries, rather than candidate counts.


Experimental rigor and validation
Uses fixed audit pools, multiple randomized orderings (20 per checkpoint), and four seeds to mitigate variance, reporting both matched-budget and same-candidate curves.
Evaluates across two datasets and two privacy regimes with careful public/private splits, and details a conservative transform-screening procedure.
Provides ablations (no residualization, removing agreement/worst components, K=2 views) and analyzes the λ-selection pathology, showing that λ often collapses to 0 under the ε objective.


Clarity of presentation
Threat model, metrics, and evaluation protocol are stated unambiguously; the distinction between matched-budget and same-candidate views is helpful and fair.
Tables and figures are well-organized and consistently report mean and variability; the negative result is presented transparently.
Reproducibility details (environment, seeds, deviations) are unusually thorough for a negative-result paper.


Significance of contributions
Offers a clear operational takeaway for practitioners in compute-bounded one-run audits: investing queries in additional weak views may not translate into tighter ε lower bounds when a strong per-example calibrator is already in place.
Establishes a baseline protocol (matched-total-query accounting) that others can reuse when proposing multi-query test-time scoring in one-run auditing.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
The screened weak transforms are extremely conservative (often only very small Gaussian noise passes, and only a single transform on CIFAR-10), narrowing the condition under which weak views could plausibly help.
The structured score family and aggregator (max of residuals with a scalar λ for agreement) may be too restrictive; other learned aggregations or robust statistics could be more effective.
The λ-selection degeneracy (λ = 0 under ε objective) suggests the proxy selection is underpowered; this partly undermines the evaluation of agreement-based signals.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
Limited scope: two datasets, two ε targets, and four seeds; CIFAR-10 uses a frozen encoder with a DP linear head, not full end-to-end DP training.
No exploration of more diverse or realistic label-preserving transforms (e.g., small translations/rotations, color jitter, blur), stochastic test-time noise/dropout, or calibration of “weakness” via controlled label-stability instead of fixed thresholds.
Power analysis is missing; many ε lower bounds are near zero, raising the question of whether the audits are underpowered to detect small effects from multi-view aggregation.


Clarity or presentation issues
While the protocol is clear, explicit confidence intervals or formal tests for differences between methods would strengthen claims about “no sustained improvement.”
Some choices (e.g., denominator floor τt in residualization) are described briefly; more intuition on stability and its impact would help.


Missing related work or comparisons
No comparison to alternative one-run scoring or conversion families beyond the quantile baseline (e.g., histogram-based auditing of TV/Hockey-stick divergence or recent sequential MMD-based testers), which could contextualize whether weak views underperform broadly or only in the specific ROC-threshold ε conversion used.
While related repeated-query MI works are cited, there is limited probing of regimes where those methods succeed (e.g., richer augmentation families, adaptive querying) under matched budgets.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The matched-total-query evaluation is an appropriate and often overlooked fairness condition; equating consumed queries rather than candidates is a sound choice and central to the paper’s conclusion.
The all-public-data calibration and selection steps avoid leakage, aligning with the threat model and strengthening validity.
The finding that residualization is essential (removing it worsens ε lower bounds) corroborates Liu et al. (2025) and indicates the baseline is strong; the method failing to improve over it is a meaningful negative result.
However, the weak transform screening may be so conservative that it eliminates the very variability multi-views are supposed to exploit, making it unclear whether the conclusion generalizes.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The audit pool size (240 pairs) with multiple orderings is reasonable, but the frequent zero empirical ε lower bounds suggest the setup may be statistically underpowered; including power calculations or larger pools would increase confidence.
The same-candidate analysis is a nice diagnostic; the fact that even there gains are fleeting and vanish by budget 240 supports the central claim.
Reporting both AUC and ε lower bounds is helpful: the divergence between modest AUC gains and no ε gains under matched budgets is an important cautionary note for the community.
The λ-selection instability across objectives (ε vs. AUC) is informative; it indicates that proxy training keyed to the privacy objective remains a challenge and deserves more methodological attention.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
Relative to Liu et al. (2025), this study confirms that quantile-calibrated single-view MI remains a tough baseline to beat in the one-run, final-model-only setting, especially once matched-budget accounting is enforced.
Prior work on repeated queries and label-only MI (e.g., Choquette-Choo et al., Wu et al.) demonstrated benefits in different setups; this paper clarifies that those benefits need not translate when audits are strictly budgeted and signals are calibrated.
Alternative auditing tools, such as histogram-based TV/Hockey-stick estimation (Koskela & Mohammadi, 2024) or sequential MMD auditors (González et al., 2025), are not directly compared; including at least a brief experiment or rationale would contextualize whether the null effect of weak views is specific to the ROC-to-ε pipeline or general across auditors.
The results are orthogonal to near-tight black-box strategies (Annamalai & De Cristofaro, 2024) and adversarial sample crafting (Yoon et al., 2024); these emphasize training-time or audit design rather than test-time multi-viewing, but a short discussion of how matched-query accounting might affect those pipelines would be useful.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The work provides an actionable, empirically grounded caution: in compute-bounded one-run auditing, allocating budget to better single-view calibration and audit-pool construction may dominate spending on repeated weak test-time queries.
It encourages more rigorous budgeting/accounting in proposing new auditing variants and underscores the importance of public-only calibration in final-model-only threat models.
As a negative result with careful methodology, it sets a high bar for future multi-view proposals: they should (i) pass matched-budget comparisons, (ii) include stronger, diverse transforms, and (iii) demonstrate improvements on the actual privacy metric (ε lower bound), not just AUC.
sections.questions_for_authors:
How sensitive are the conclusions to the transform-screening thresholds? If you relax agreement requirements (e.g., to 95–97%) or include small geometric/photometric perturbations, do weak views begin to help under matched budgets?
Did you try stochastic test-time views such as MC dropout, temperature scaling, or test-time noise injected into intermediate activations (if available in black-box) to diversify signals beyond near-identity Gaussian noise?
Could a budget-adaptive strategy that spends extra queries only on candidates near the decision threshold (based on the calibrated clean residual) improve matched-budget performance?
How do results change with a larger audit pool (e.g., 1k+ pairs) or K = 8 views (not executed)? A small power analysis quantifying detectable effect sizes would help interpret the many near-zero ε lower bounds.
Would a learned public-data meta-aggregator (e.g., logistic regression over per-view residuals and agreement statistics) outperform the max-aggregation, or is it also collapsed by proxy selection under ε?
Can you report confidence intervals (or paired statistical tests) for differences between matched-budget curves to strengthen the “no sustained advantage” claim?
Do conclusions persist under alternative auditing conversions (e.g., histogram-based TV/Hockey-stick divergence or f-DP curve auditing), or is the null effect specific to the ROC-threshold-to-ε pipeline?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    4.7/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This is a careful, well-executed negative-result study answering a focused and practically relevant question in one-run, final-model-only DP auditing: do repeated weak test-time views help once queries are honestly accounted for and a strong quantile-calibrated single-view baseline is in place? The answer, under the authors’ conservative and compute-bounded setup, is consistently no. The matched-total-query protocol is a valuable contribution in its own right, and the empirical analysis is transparent, with ablations and diagnostics that support the main conclusion.
The main limitations are scope and transform diversity. The conservative screening drastically narrows the weak-view regime (especially on CIFAR-10), potentially suppressing the very effects under test; the audits also seem underpowered at times. Moreover, the structured aggregator is simple and the proxy selection degenerates, leaving open whether richer aggregators or more diverse weak views could help under matched budgets. Finally, comparisons to alternative auditing conversions would better contextualize generality.
Overall, I view this as a solid, if modest, contribution that will be useful to practitioners designing one-run audits and to researchers proposing multi-query scoring methods. Its novelty is limited and the results are narrow in scope, but the methodological care and clarity make it a worthwhile addition. I lean toward borderline acceptance, contingent on the authors expanding sensitivity analyses (transform diversity, power) and clarifying statistical support for the null results.
